@positivepolly06 Mine was about 3 months to notice something was really wrong; initially got sick in March 2020, thought I recovered, but every time I had some everyday sorts of life stressors (work, moving, etc) I started having worsening chronic symptoms that I assumed were just burnout

@positivepolly06 Then June/July 2020 I was *really* tired and decided to take time to do extremely little and rest up. But it never got better, and then when I started an internship in August 2020 the cognitive symptoms were so pronounced I knew it was beyond burnout. After getting a bunch

@positivepolly06 Of negative test results on so many panels of bloodwork, talking with docs, and doing my own research became pretty clear case of post viral fatigue/CFS due to COVID. Had several health crashes over the year after that trying to push myself to work but my body finally couldn’t

@positivepolly06 Take it. I’m lucky that I’m still in the mild end of the spectrum, I can go to the store and cook and bathe and such, and a modest coaching business seems feasible at the moment (working on a website right now), but scared I could get worse

@louis02x Depends on how systematic ppl are about it and what other theories influence their approach. As a therapist I’d generally be on the lookout for a sign of clear internal conflict (usually inner critic is an easy start, esp if client is new to therapy). I use a lot of somatic

@louis02x Elements, eg find its sensation in the body, what’s its texture/sensation like, etc. Then typically have clients put the part in a chair across from them and engage in dialogue (eg “Ask this part of you ‘What do you do for me?’”) while attending to emotional activation

@louis02x At some point have the client switch chairs and take the perspective of the part and look back at their “experiencing self.” Continuing with some dialogue and reflection before bringing client back to their own seat. The goal here is to 1) make emotional tension between parts

@louis02x Less intense 2) develop recognition of internal dialogues and make them more explicit + friendly/collaborative 3) identify the part that’s being protected by the more vocal/loud part 4) learn to meet the needs of the difficult part in a healthy way so the protected part
@louis02x Can be met and invited in safely
@louis02x Depending on client’s level of insight and experience you may be able to do a lot of this in a single session but often it occurs over multiple

One of the primary hacks for confidence is warmth/care. Focus less on getting things right or performing well than projecting warmth and care for your task, seeking to offer what’s most beneficial rather than what’s most “right”
The practice of warmth and care for your projects or relationships or whatever will typically teach you just as deeply as seeking out mastery, but it’ll be more sustainable and pleasurable

This practice is one of cultivation as opposed to manipulation. You can simply practice holding yourself with warmth and care while offering that felt sense of caring attention to whatever you’re doing

This takes some practice often, because capitalism and the current cultural moment contorts our cares and passions into particular shapes that make our practices of warmth and care relatively shallow, instead pushing for productivity and performance of narrow scripts

Warmth and care are art forms; they start with getting to know what the most foundational aspects of warmth and care are like, what it’s like to hold yourself and the many phenomena in your unfolding life path with curiosity as you lean into the practice of, well, love.

I’ll also add, true warmth and care do not conflict with other important qualities like fierceness or strength. Cultivating a life with warmth as a central quality allows for deep discernment with ferocity and strength https://t.co/S3bMDSIKbU

Even gentleness, which can sound far too soft, is an often arduous practice. You might worry if you’re “gentle” with yourself, or if others are gentle with themselves, they’ll never grow or mature past unwanted emotional reactions.

But it’s the very harshness we treat ourselves with that can turn a trigger into a panic attack. Warmth, care, gentleness in the most radical, true senses meet everything directly, allowing for grace to arise and make for new pathways

A lot of the reason people don’t like meditation is they hear/take instruction in a way that amplifies constriction (or the instructor themself is constructed). Practice should be a *friendly direct connection* with experience as opposed to a *strict discipline*

Ways to get into meditation more smoothly: allow for movement to happen throughout practice; attune to the senses in way that orients to natural, non-addictive pleasure; less “focus” and more attunement (eg “allow the body to breathe in the way that feels most pleasant” as

Opposed to “focusing” on the breath).

The *just right* amount of scaffolding for good practice brings you into direct contact with experience while also allowing practice to become increasingly agenda-less (and strict focusing can be really agenda-ful)

One of the best spiritual pointings I’ve heard was from Dare Sohei, they teach on non dual animism, and what they said was something like, “Animism isn’t so much about anthropomorphising the world around you, but de-anthropomorphising yourself” and it’s with me every day https://t.co/H8j3irbaz6

The more I practice and the more I work artfully with thought forms the more clearly I perceive/experience how interwoven everything is in one fabric, and I don’t even think these words are all that adequate. It’s almost overwhelming how little I know yet how far I’ve come
It’s all incredibly beautiful tho, and it’s a powerful shortcut for under-cutting a lot of the neurotic dramas that consumed my mind for a long time (they still do sometimes but they’re quieter now)

You start to recognize thought forms as creating false images of the world (including your self-image), and then when you start to live in full sensory contact with the world the aliveness of it all is just, well, different. And it’s not that you were ever separate from it

I don’t have much more brain juice for waxing non-dual, but the very life force that animates your sense of sight, your sense of touch, is animating everything else as far as I can tell, and it’s really something to behold when you get out of the way

Co-regulation is so incredibly overpowered for personal growth and spiritual/emotional breakthroughs, I know the authentic relating world has its issues but as the social meditation/AR practices get refined I foresee a massive relational tech upgrade

It’s like, if you put just the right scaffolding on social connection, all the sudden you can rest in bliss, or find spontaneous play, or move through sticky emotions, all with a level of real time consent that’s impossible with normal conversational scripts
When you experience embodied communication with clean and clear emotional boundaries, the magic just unfolds on its own
I’m so excited about this, I’m so glad I get to do this work, even if life has given me very challenging circumstances

Doing this sort of work has led me to become a bit of a couples/relationship specialist; the cultural scripts and emotional intelligences we’ve inherited really limit intimacy and interpersonal nourishment https://t.co/NDRf7qogns

I also make it a point, even when the person I work with starts as a 1-on-1 client, that my model is to have them bring a loved one or friend to sessions so I can teach them how to co-regulate together so they eventually have a robust network of intimacy

1) this works me out of a job in a healthy way 2) this is what’s missing from most couples therapy; you might learn communication skills and work through relational challenges but nobody’s really doing deep embodied connection work, the kind that makes it an easy practice
With the right practices (and honest investment from those involved), you can get a depth of easy emotional/relational connection in 15-20 minutes of practice that many people never taste imho

One of the limitations of talk therapy is that the practice is built on *talking about* life as opposed to direct engagement with the process of living as it’s happening, which is just really inefficient. Of course some models are more experiential or process-oriented, but those

Don’t generalize into normal human relationships directly. There are skills that are helpful, and being witnessed in particular ways is a big part of healing. Somatic modalities are seeking to address a lot of this, and I think with the proliferation of therapy, more clients who

Have done the basic skills work (or see through the shallow bullshit that gets peddled by a lot of coaches/therapists), and the incoming wave of AI emotional support, the leading edge will be real time attuned somatic work.

It’s amazing what happens when you get good at releasing contraction. Suddenly it’s easy to notice when I’m assuming a potential emotional reaction in others and let go of it, trusting that I can navigate any reaction when it becomes apparent as opposed to assuming or worrying

This also works with desire. You contract around what you assume will fulfill your yearning and desire at some future point, and when you get good at releasing contraction you can live into organic nourishment.

You can’t get your deepest desires met by thinking and controlling your way there. If they’re coming in thought forms, they’re ideas placed on top of your embodiment and are by definition shallow. When your in a state of natural unfolding, depth comes in often surprising ways

The important point here is that what we assume to be real is just an energetic contraction, and when we let go of the underlying assumption of its reality we get to be more in touch with the fundamental aliveness animating this whole thing https://t.co/z6E91kbMTO

Might as well talk about resistance too. If you meet unpleasant sensations or thoughts with resistance, you simply create more internal tension. Meeting them with benevolent awareness allows for much more spaciousness (*and efficiency* as much as it’s possible)

*HOWEVER,* it’s a trap to assume that all resistance is something *bad* to avoid. That’s just becoming resistant to resistance. When you’re artful and discerning, you can orient to nourishing and feeding the things you love (people, family, community, projects, causes, etc)…
And then you may *run into resistance* in the field that impedes nourishment/care/health. This is where healthy resistance lies; it takes the form of discernment

Discernment, that is,  between useless resistance (fighting with the *idea* of reality) vs healthy resistance (creatively approaching real world problems). Anything too far to one side (being hypervigilant about resisting issues vs never resisting) are naive imho

It’s generally a bad idea to assume someone’s intent, good or bad. Intent is murkier than we assume, it’s possible to have multiple intents, & it’s often impossible to know intent. The more you interpret and reinterpret what someone says/does, you’re getting further from reality

It’s not uncommon for someone to have a partner who is emotionally abusive/manipulative/contemptuous yet consistently reinterpret their statements (eg “Well, what they really meant was…”) or redirect (eg “Well I should be grateful for x aspect of the relationship”)

A direct assessment is better. Eg 1) how does it feel in my body to be around them? 2) If I saw a stranger say this to a friend of mine, what would I think? 3) How can I communicate my needs or establish boundaries (dependent upon safety/the person’s emotional competence)?
But this all first depends on actually feeling what you feel, allowing yourself to acknowledge how you’re actually impacted and acknowledging that ppl are responsible for their actions.

There’s a dangerous common phrase that gets used by some therapists/healers (and if you watched the docs about NXIVM it gets used by cults too) that says “No one can make you feel anything, it’s how you react/respond”. Ppl of course can make you feel things,  no one says this…

In response to positive feelings. It should instead be stated “Yes people can make you feel things, *and* you take your power back in how you respond to those feelings.” Where it gets dangerous is that, after the fact it’s easy to convince yourself that your feelings weren’t that

Big of a deal. A mediocre therapist/healer, or a manipulative cult leader, will often say things like “No one can make you feel anything,” and then things like eg a shallow version of empathy to “take a look from their pov” and assume a false equivalence in contribution to probs
This might seem relatively benign (and it can be), but it can also set people up to develop really really entrenched habits of invalidating their real time emotional signals

And there are certainly cases where people externalize all blame for their uncomfortable feelings, but it’s dangerous to meet that with the falsehood that “No one can make you feel anything.” If you teach that to someone that becomes a parent, they might then emotionally harm

Perhaps *the* fundamental question is “What am I orienting to?” Our typical operating mode is “Something about me/others/the world is wrong,” essentially a fundamental assumption that leads us into a constant state of reactivity and an orientation to needing to fix/change

The healthier version starts with orienting to wholesomeness/nourishment/ok-ness/neutrality in our sense field, cultivating well-being where we can, and then any barriers to well-being (whether for self/others/the world) reveal themselves more clearly.
From this place, we can still address problems, but from a place of resource and creativity as opposed to depletion and reactivity.

It’s important that this orientation start with the senses, because often we’re orienting to our *idea* of the world/self/relationships instead of where our actual connectedness lies. If you try to fix your *idea* of self/others/the world, you’re just gonna be hurt when…

Your beliefs don’t match what’s actually there. 

Start with nourishment, and follow the path that suits *you* best. Of course, be willing to look and see if it’s actual nourishment or if it’s intoxication/the idea of nourishment.

I recommend orienting to nourishment first so you’re well resourced if you run into difficulty on the emotional/relational/spiritual journey. And it’s also one of the most important indicators of a good healer: direct orientation to developing *your* capacity to feel ok

Whether it’s feeling rested, supported, etc, you should feel a development of attunement to your own nervous system. Good signs are feelings of spaciousness, softening, strength that doesn’t feel like a pose/posture (eg your spine feeling aligned and stronger but not stiff)

Bad signs are healers that talk *at* you a lot, teach you to invalidate sensory/emotional information, or ones that do shit that you can’t feel and don’t communicate with you (eg a buncha energy workers that are just playing shadow puppets in their heads)

Healing doesn’t always feel “good,” but it’s a hella bad sign if someone doesn’t have skills that teach you to feel good in your nervous system *in real time.* You should be invited skillfully into your experience and have a sense of being able to rest there
And I define “rest” here expansively. People’s systems are different so you should be supported in discovering what feels restful, what feels generative, what feels playful, what feels strong.
There’s the healers out there who do half-baked spiritual/energy work, and then there’s also the traditional western therapists who’s inner world is stiff as a board and you can feel it.

Think of how you approach exercise. It’s almost always, I have this goal, I need to push to become better, I want to compete with these people, etc. Rarely does it orient, in real time, to “How do I feel good in my nervous system?” https://t.co/5viJlP19be

@triciapickren Hehehe right? I’ve come to enjoy/appreciate the label of coach/consultant. It really works for me and how I work, because I teach a lot of emotional agility and body awareness then support people to taking it into relationships.

That it’s natural doesn’t mean that it comes easy, or there won’t be dark/trauma-related stuff to move through before it feels natural. But the process should feel like a natural unfolding, orienting to nourishment then processing the phenomena that impede natural goodness

Attuning to the textures and physics and improvisational aspects of intimacy and emotion are more foundational than story and philosophy and interpretation when it comes to building healthy relationships

One of the early insights I’ve supported a lot of younger people reckoning with is the difference between *having* love and *practicing* love. Usually when navigating a painful relationship with a parent or romantic partner. It was one of the most important early insights for me

So often people do genuinely *have* love for us but are also unable to practice loving behavior, and holding the genuine complexity of that is essential for both stewarding the trajectory of that relationship along with developing emotional health

Imho you should never assume intent in either direction, good or bad. Where I see people end up in bad places is when they get into really shallow forms of “positive thinking” eg “Well what they were really trying to say was x” from an assumption that a person was trying to be

Loving. Instead, just a sober look at what their behavior demonstrates without editorializing really gives clear info on how close you can be to a person. There’s so much more I could unpack with this but my brain doesn’t have much more juice for fleshing this one out rn 😌

This. When you find a barrier or constriction or confusion, the way to growth isn’t to conquer or eliminate it, it’s to hold it with grace and loving awareness. After all, it’s part(s) of you that’s been shaped that way and it’s reaction makes sense https://t.co/P7o7hSylnN

If you “fight” with internal difficulties it only creates more tension. Meeting constrictions and barriers and confusions with love means the parts that have been harmed or neglected or malnourished can become healthy as opposed to contorted around

One of my first spiritual friends I made when I started my journey shared a bit of wisdom “When your demons arrive, bake them cookies.” Of course all of this should be done with discernment, but all psycho-emotional-spiritual-relational difficulties need to be met lovingly*
*caveat here that “lovingly” is meant with the full complexity and wisdom required for spiritual-emotional reckoning, not shallow ideas of love

Wisdom and knowledge are fundamentally different. And the most frustrating thing I found in academic psychology, especially in the realm of psychotherapy and the human condition, was that it usually tries to apply knowledge practices where wisdom is needed

@thecaptain_nemo Yeah, there are pitfalls, Buddhism can lead to people being thrown into the deep end before they can swim/tread water. I personally think that people should approach healing/reckoning with this life with a relational approach first, such that they develop nourishing connections

@thecaptain_nemo With some grounding in consensus reality. Perhaps it doesn’t have to be first but at least concurrently. This helps people to gently meet the very confusing and possibly unsettling elements at a healthy pace.

At the most basic level, it’s much more important to have the foundational skills of somatically meeting your lived experience with caring awareness than it is to have cognitive forms of self-knowledge. Caring/loving awareness is a wisdom practice at its core

Unconditional love isn’t something you *do* or others *do.* Its the fundamental nature of reality, and the ways most people tap into it is when we are in loving conditions in the relative world. Loving conditions, at core,don’t bring us happiness in having or receiving something,

They allow us to touch into a sense of being completely free of having or needing anything at all, everything is right just as it is. Good spiritual-emotional paths point us to holding reality in such a way that tapping into that which is unconditional becomes more natural
When you repeatedly practice orienting to aspects of experience that are unconditional, you recognize that your thought based sense of emotional-spiritual-relational well-being is conditional.

We often feel afraid to let go of the thoughts and beliefs about what conditions would help us feel loved, but the practice of creating those thoughts and believing them creates a conditional sense of love. Orienting to the unconditional, we 1) feel liberation from our mind’s

Constant searching for right/wrong, good/bad that makes our well-being feel incredibly precarious, and 2) find that we naturally gravitate towards better emotional-relational conditions as we are better attuned to the emotional-relational field

This doesn’t mean we disengage with the processes of navigating the relative conditions of our lives and try to build a more compassionate and loving world. It actually just makes that practice easier to engage with, because instead of being so caught up in *ideas,* we live in
Direct connection with what is and find ourselves doing what life is calling forth from us.

Caveat here that this unconditional love isn’t one that means we come to a place where we only feel warm and pleasant things. It’s that we tap into the deepest wisdoms, which are actually the most natural when we find our selves humbly and dedicatedly engaged with this life

Occasionally I see takes against radical gentleness, assuming it’s some sort of spiritual bypassing (which sure it can be if used in certain ways). But to be truly, radically gentle with all of your experience brings you into direct contact with all the shadow places

To be radically gentle also brings you into direct contact with all that you are in connection with. It’s actually a quite intense and you could say violent process to rip yourself away from connection and into cognitive ideas about what the world/this life is
You can’t really reckon deeply with the emotional-relational-spiritual aspects of life without being radically gentle, holding everything in the most basic forms of compassion

The trick to avoiding bypassing is a simple practice (simple in terms of instructions, often difficult in execution): just simply ask yourself “What am I avoiding right now?” And hold whatever arises, including confusion about whether you’re avoiding at all, as gently as possible https://t.co/EmRNEhRHds
If you find this difficult, or there’s really sticky or triggering material, that’s where you seek support from friends/guides/teachers/therapists/etc.

One of the points of this isn’t that you have to police yourself into constant “authenticity;” simply noticing what those differences are and where they are most pronounced will help you discover deeper authenticity and which relationships allow space for it https://t.co/E5m0wzoOjh

What I’ve found is that struggle and strain to be authentic isn’t the most fruitful approach because it’s deeply affected by the relational field you’re embedded within, so just approach with gentle curiosity and you’ll naturally cultivate wisdom

It’s really bananas just how fertile the emotional-relational ground is and just how little it’s been cultivated; I think that’s why we’re seeing such exponential growth in people exploring these realms and making life projects from it

From my exp/education, seeing how emotionally bereft the field of psychotherapy has been historically (and honestly still is), and working across generations (everything from being assistant director of an aging clinic to working in community mental health with children)…

It becomes abundantly clear that we’ve barely scratched the surface. The very foundations and scaffoldings that create how we communicate and think about our lived emotional-relational experience are, well, generally skewed and contorted, and finding domains that truly invite
Authentic and nourishing forms of connection is rare but becoming less so imho.

Just seeing what my parents and their friends and my older adult clients and the peer counselors I trained thought emotional health was like and their structures of thought around mental health, it was, well, it made a lot of sense why their range was so limited. Not to say this
Was universally the case, just that it was the prevailing emotional culture and helps to make sense of what we’re reckoning with now.

I try to emphasize it a lot, especially y in my work, but there’s one important core truth that should be evident from anyone who’s doing emotional-relational-spiritual work: the best any teacher/therapist/coach/mentor/etc can do is *invite you* onto your own path

If you don’t feel empowered - and instead you feel like you’re just replacing old expectations with new ones, or creating a more gentle confinement, or just re-interpreting your life in a nicer way - then it’s prob time to find different support imho

Useful experiment time, hearted-centered edition: try practicing speaking from and listening with your heart. And this is a literally physiological practice; drop the center of your awareness from your head to your heart in convo with say, a friend, and…

Notice what’s different if you patiently and curiously try to speak and listen from your heart. There’s particular authentic relating and social meditation practices that are good for this, but it’s also something you can try on your own

Important distinction: I mean speaking spontaneously from the heart, from what’s organically arising. Sometimes we internalize saccharine and idealized senses of what it means to speak from the heart; it won’t necessarily be particularly emotional! Just play 😉

@hermittoday Yeah for sure. The foundation I typically start with (which can be the whole practice) is just a somatic-emotional form of the noticing game (an authentic relating game). The basic format is someone starts with “Being in connection with you I feel _____” and states their current

@hermittoday Feeling/sensations without story or explanation, and then the other person (can be done in pairs or small groups) shares back “Hearing that I feel ____” again without story or explanation. Then it continues as an organic unfolding process. It’s really good for co-regulation

@hermittoday And gaining insight into attachment patterns and navigating intimacy through good times and through conflict. I usually start just with the noticing piece then add on offers or invitations, where participants can eg initiate touch or ask for support in creative ways, one

@hermittoday Relational move at a time. Another add in is welcoming story and invitation but only in single chunks, like one or two sentences at a time, while still speaking first from feeling or sensation. A lot of where we get into trouble is through the fact that we make multiple moves
@hermittoday In quick succession without slowing down to attend to impact or notice when we’re out of alignment.

@hermittoday The format isn’t strict, so breaking the “rules” doesn’t really matter, they’re just there so we orient to what’s most alive in the present moment and speak from there, and also so emotional boundaries are clear.

@hermittoday Also if you’re ever interested in practicing I’m always looking to share it and build friendships/practice buddy-ships, if you need references @danielbrottman and @triciapickren seem to think I’m ok (for now 😇)

Spiritual practice + self-growth should generally feel like a creative, artful practice, something that’s bringing you into the path that could only be yours. If it doesn’t, take a look at your approach and/or the people supporting you and bring your most graceful curiosity to it

You might be asking: how can I know what “my most graceful curiosity” is??

Well, you’re going to have so much fun finding out 😌😇😌
Just get curious about your *real time* experience - ie, with whom, in what situations, doing what, do I feel most connected to sustainably nourishing sensations? What brings out the best in me?

On the converse, get curious about where the constriction and stagnancy in your life is - ie with whom, in what situations, doing what, do I feel most contorted or on edge or stuck? What brings out the worst in me or makes me feel like I have to battle all the time

And the trick here is - let go of agenda. Welcome whatever shows up with grace and allow wisdom to cultivate naturally. You’ll be surprised by what resources you’ll be able to cultivate and the new dance moves you’ll have, but that takes some measure of trust in yourself

Trusting yourself might be hard, but I can guarantee you one thing: 

I believe in you 

😘

Brief note on going agenda-less:

This is a practice of letting go but not forcefully pushing away. There’s nothing to do, but also nothing to avoid doing. The practice of surrendering so deeply that the only thing left is the organic unfolding of your divine will. https://t.co/KNFwulW16Z

It’s a lot less important that you generate gratitude than finding out what it’s organic, embodied sense is like so you can recognize when you’re genuinely touched by it.
We’re used to cultural scripts influenced by the notion that you “should” be grateful, which can (and often is) just self-manipulation.

Gratitude fundamentally isn’t something you *do,* it’s a natural emotional reaction. So when trying to tap into it, it’s better to connect with a sense of where it’s already existing than trying to construct it.

Eg connecting with a sense of a loving parent/friend/etc and
Enjoying the felt sense of being nourished by that relationship.

Because gratitude is subject to so much manipulation by authority figures, you end up somewhat ironically with one of the most important skills for gratitude being the ability to recognize where it doesn’t exist even tho sociocultural messaging tells you it “should”
RT @visakanv: the way to get good at something is practice and study. ~95% of the time when someone asks “how did that person get so good a…

Now, all this doesn’t exclude us from doing things *with* the gratitude that we experience, it’s just when I hear people say that gratitude is a skill I want to unpack it. There’s a difference between experiencing it and expressing/sharing it, and some of the fundamentals are

Missed by our current sociocultural scaffolding around embodied emotional experience (I should note too that thoughts and/or feelings that happen in our headspace aren’t necessarily disembodied, but it’s important to be curious if we’re actually experiencing gratitude or just
expressing scripted ideas we’ve inherited)

Gratitude thoughts for thanksgiving today.

When I lead guided meditations that include gratitude practice, my instructions aren’t to think of things you’re grateful for. Instead I prompt people to connect with the gratitude that already exists in their experience… https://t.co/BjrXp5UOI1

First connecting with gratitude in relationship with all the beings that have nurtured and supported you, then gratitude in relationship with yourself, then gratitude in relationship with all the beings you get to love and nurture.

The intentions behind doing it this way is to undercut the pressure to *create* gratitude and instead find its natural occurrence. Because gratitude is highly socially manipulated, it’s important that people get a sense of the organic foundations of healthy gratitude.

Training yourself to simply “think of” things you’re grateful for can be a form of self-manipulation, especially in the context of sociocultural and familial pressures that you should feel grateful for particular people and things.

This might seem benign or good but you can create false stories of gratitude that eventually crumble when life presents you with the big emotional challenges that inevitably come. Also, having access to the embodied emotional sense of being touched by gratitude is incredibly
Important for ability to discern which relationships and domains and activities and interests really feed you

All this to say, it’s ok to not feel grateful, and reclaiming the truth of what the actual experience of gratitude is like is genuinely countercultural in a world that pressures you to feel grateful in particular ways.

Truly, if you want to know gratitude for real, you have to allow it to come to you, to arise naturally. If it’s not coming, constructing it because you think you should might provide short term relief but will have long term consequences of your emotional compass being skewed https://t.co/0kZfDT8mcE
And holy shit, when you do get to know gratitude well, it is *so fucking abundant.* Ime you’ll be moved and touched in ways you didn’t know were possible https://t.co/CBJHkDyqE3
Whew this life is really beautiful and I am grateful for all the beings in it 🥰

One of the reasons gentle medicine is *incredibly* important in healing/emotional support practices is that encouraging catharsis and acutely intense experience when people are removed from their community and support networks can be really jarring and the insights may be

More disconnected from the foundations of people’s lives.

There’s a lot of really intense practice out there, but that typically doesn’t do the work of sinking gently into healthy intimacy with one’s life, which takes a graceful, patient, daily practice

That’s not at all to say that catharsis and acutely intense healing experiences aren’t valuable, but having healthy outcomes with them is much more likely with a foundation of wisdom and emotional poise/grace.

This is really important to me as a professional, like I prioritize helping people find the truest sense of gentleness/compassion with the hope that they find catharsis naturally and healthily with their loved ones as opposed to in a one on one session with me https://t.co/HrgTGkl406

I never suppress or discourage natural emotional processes so if cathartic things happen with me I’m very welcoming, and the gentle medicine approach builds the type of foundation that leads to more healthily calibrated and distributed catharsis imho

When people get good support in learning how to hold themselves compassionately, gently, and with curiosity, they develop wisdom for growing towards nourishing relationships, creating the opportunities for mutually nurturing care that brings the healthiest catharsis

This is also why, whenever I work with someone, the structure I use eventually encourages them to bring a loved one/friend/collaborator to get basic co-regulation, attunement, and generative conversation practices down, so they learn how to do the skills I teach them with their

One manifestation of this “what’s wrong” orientation is what I would call “brittle positivity;” which is an attitude of “everything is ok as long as I always avoid discomfort.” It’s a facsimile of ok-ness through control https://t.co/5viJlP19be

You can see this in attitudes eg “I can always re-interpret my partner’s or parents’ actions to be loving” or “I’m ok as long as I don’t waver from my exercise routine” or just when you ask someone how they’re doing and they say “Great” through gritted teeth.

It takes a cultivation of wisdom and authenticity to discover the ways in which ok-ness is always available, and this in fact isn’t just about being ok with discomfort but discovering that our avoidance of it in fact makes well-being much more difficult

All this to say, when you get good with discomfort, it can be neutral or even positive to ecstatic in experience. *And* that doesn’t mean we just endure acute pain or beat ourselves with it (you should honor your organic and creative action that meets discomfort with grace) https://t.co/m7Um8FWsf9

The more you work with it, the more you discover the subtle reactivity that adds tension on top of discomfort (discomfort with the fact of discomfort). When you start releasing your contraction around discomfort, it becomes much more malleable and dynamic

Also, the more you let go of *defining* the magic or trying to hold onto it as a memory or thing you can take with you, the more abundantly available it is imho

RT @grantbels: tao te ching: “do not have intention in order to see the wonder / always have intention in order to see the forms.” holding…

This q/t from QC helps me to get a bit more clear about the underlying process here and exactly what I mean. Orienting to ok-ness is never about denying wrong-ness, nor your natural impulses towards action (I’m going to draw a lot from Organic Intelligence here) https://t.co/aynhmE8MKx

Orientation is simply the practice of being intentional in how we habitually turn towards life. (Perhaps the ultimate aspiration is realizing the truth of “neither intention nor not-intention” in how we orient but that’s another conversation 😂)

At the present collective moment, there’s an over-abundance of “what’s wrong” orientation; meaning there’s a hyper-vigilance toward what is, was, or could be wrong. This leading to chronic nervous system dysregulation, self-loathing and cynicism, and more.
(You could call a “what’s wrong” orientation being a manifestation of insecure attachment with life)

The way to find a healthier orientation is simply to practice orienting to what’s ok/neutral/pleasant in the present moment and learning to hang in regular contact with these things. This is very much about sensory engagement and embodiment (incl relating to thought as a sense)

Bringing us back to the important point of not denying wrongness: orienting to ok-ness will make true wrong-nesses more apparent (and also much more clear than when wrong-ness is a generalized patina projected over everything)

The healthy process is one of orientation-differentiation-integration. Orienting establishes a connection with ok-ness. Which allows a clearer ability to differentiate between what feels healthy and good vs what needs nurturing support or action of some type

That sense of differentiation and meeting what’s here with creativity and compassionate curiosity allows them to be integrated in the whole as opposed to needing to push away or manipulate the bad parts compulsively

When you have a fundamental orientation of wrongness, it’s like a generalized “no” to life, and often that constricts your ways of feeling good to one’s where you forget about yourself or life and then when you return your still left not ok

When you practice a true orientation to ok-ness, there’s a harnessing of wholesome and sustainable ways to feel good, and because you’ll meet them from greater resource, you can address wrongnesses with much more spaciousness for creativity and minimizing harm to self and other

I also think this is a gentle way to introduce people to embodied emotional experience and contemplative practice. Instead of just dropping people in, directing people in ways like “Just notice what in your experience feels good/neutral/ok already” and help them to get skillful

At some easier wholesome states and they might naturally be drawn to pursue spiritual-emotional work that is more intense

(I’ll emphasize it’s important to let go of agenda wrt the intensity of emotional work aside from the agenda of safety + consent)

@QiaochuYuan I think we might not actually disagree on this point either. Like when I say “fundamental” I mean holding onto a core sense of a “no” to life generally. When even this is met with ok-ness as it is, ppl are free to simply live what life is calling forth from them (ie divine will)

Emotional-relational-spiritual problems arise from stuckness, inability to grow and change. And if you spend much time with yourself, you’ll witness yourself play through repetitive, agenda-laden patterns, so much so that you can see your own reactive scripts play out predictably

And one addl note, you should be cultivating *your own* wisdom, so don’t take anything I say as gospel. The best wisdom is whatever invites you most gracefully into your own life.

I’ve probably seen 4-5 therapists for therapy stints ranging from 6 months to a couple years. By far the most profound change came from 5 free sessions with a somatic therapist who was seeing his first clients (it did help that I had years of spiritual practice under my belt) https://t.co/WDWZSdR1t9

What was most powerful about it was that it was just gentle and direct invitation into connection with both that which was nourishing/supportive and that which was challenging/painful. When you let go of story/labels and just meet the embodied phenomena, there’s incredible room

For organic and spontaneous healing/growth to take place. The guy I worked with just did real basic, bare bones stuff (was a student at Naropa, which I think helped bc it was less formulaic, had fewer “somatic exercises” that you get from western psych style training)

Had *really* profound spiritual movements, tapped into capacities for embodied nourishment I didn’t know I had, and just had more of my curiosity and vibrancy encouraged than in the often stilted format of talk therapy.

I think talk therapy is often stilted, flat, and/or harmful because we haven’t really interrogated the foundations of our language and social scripts. There’s so many power moves and forced ways of relating that are non-consensual embedded in these things, and good somatic… https://t.co/VwrXcrcKGS

Imho:

World needs more non-hierarchical mutual support that draws on wisdom and embodiment practices, and they need to be delivered from anti-cult, anti-guru frames.

Anti-cult and anti-guru are simply notions of practice that any person seeking to be a proponent of a healthier socio-emotional landscape needs to invite others into their own power instead of elevating figures and groups and institutions. Distribution of power for fertile soil

This is easier said than done, mostly because the ways we’ve been taught to relate to ourselves and to groups/scenes/institutions is to build up and protect the ideas and images of what these things are as opposed to nurturing a vibrant field that can meet needs dynamically

To me this means leaning into non-hierarchical practices. Not to say that hierarchy is inherently bad, it’s in fact very necessary and healthy when practiced with wisdom and discernment. But instead to have people actually experience what it’s like to be in true non-hierarchy so

They’re attuned to power as it shifts and moves and undulates. 

So much more here in terms of emotional-relational intelligence.

@PhenomLighter @QiaochuYuan This is a common misconception. Anger *can* be a secondary emotion, but it can also be a primary adaptive emotion. Also other emotions can be secondary to anger when you’re relationship with anger has been manipulated/suppressed

Anger is an emotion where I find the concept of Emotional Hegemony super helpful (coined by Alison Jaggar in the paper Love and Knowledge: Emotion in Feminist Epistemology). It’s the notion that emotional expression is contorted around power dynamics at various levels, especially https://t.co/d2pIWzAnNy
Pointing to the collective level but also useful in looking at any level of relationship and social structure as all are power-laden
Hegemonic power isn’t inherently bad; power dynamics are always part of life. For example, as QC points to good hegemonic emotional norms help limit/prevent violence

You can have different POVs on what hegemonic power looks like at the moment and who holds it in which particular ways, but the fact that emotions are contorted around power is pretty indisputable imo

Anger and gratitude are prime examples for this; they are deeply tied to not just a feeling but to a relationship(s) such that emphasizing gratitude and/or suppressing anger shapes your actions within that relationship. This is also why they’re often among the
most manipulated from childhood (“You should be grateful” “How dare you take that tone” etc)

I spent a lot of time working with young adults in their first time in therapy, and there’s so often an internalized script that plays out, as soon as they would express frustration with a parent they would rationalize with a “but I should be grateful bc…”

This is often a key indicator of internalized manipulation, that there were feelings that couldn’t be expressed without rejection/punishment/combative reaction/etc. Actually welcoming them is an act of emotional subversion (also coined by Jaggar)

This is threatening to an unhealthy family system that can’t adapt to meet the need that the unwelcome emotion is pointing to. Similarly, on a collective level, an unhealthy dynamic can’t adapt to the need collective feeling(s) among its constituents points to

On a collective level you can simply look at the military (or the US as a nation). What kind of collective pressures are placed on how people should feel about it? What feelings are welcome to be expressed publicly vs which aren’t?

Another example is motherhood. Women have enormous pressure on how they’re supposed to feel about being a mother/having children, and if they’re surrounded by ppl who uphold those norms inflexibly that can make it much more difficult when
things like post-partum depression (which often includes suppressed anger) arise

One more example, it’s not really nuanced enough to say men aren’t allowed to be vulnerable, it’s that they are only allowed to in specific circumstances, in certain ways, and with certain people, again as a product of particular collective arrangements and personal histories

The helpfulness of the notion of emotional hegemony is that it’s a good lens for seeing collective patterns. Whatever views you hold you can look at collective issues and see how particular groups, spaces, etc hold and maintain emotional norms, some healthy and some harmful

A lot of people who get heavily involved in activism end up burned out for exactly this reason; there are huuuuge amounts of emotional coercion and emotional performance happening as part of how people assert and/or challenge power

Ppl can fully lose sight of others humanity through these very processes, which is why I think developing healthy integration and expression isn’t just part of individual healing/growth but also creating healthy social ecologies and collective change

Anger as fire works as a metaphor here. When it’s welcome in healthy ways, small controlled fires burn away detritus. When anger is suppressed and the issues it points to unmet, you eventually get wildfires of escalating harm.

Ok also have to point out that this is false, because I did the reading 😉🤓😉

Anger *can* be a secondary emotion, but it can also be primary adaptive emotion. Anger is an adaptive response to seeing someone/something you care about harmed https://t.co/B9g6l2zo2r
(I can’t remember which one, but there’s a textbook on Emotion-Focused Therapy that did an in-depth dive on emotion theory)

Many emotions can be secondary to anger when you’ve experienced forms of suppression around your anger, the same way anger can be secondary to eg sadness when the vulnerability of sadness has been suppressed/repressed.

You can see how things like gender would highly impact this

I think it’s poor framing to say anger is more socially acceptable than other emotions; it’s more informative to look at how it’s particularly shaped by familial and sociocultural pressures. And any time spent working in therapy, esp w women, you’ll find that welcoming organic
Healthy connection with anger, held in the context of a rich tapestry that welcomes all emotions, is one of the most important (and honestly often poorly practiced) aspects of healing

Most therapy and coaching, as individualized containers (even “group” therapy is isolated from participants’social network most of…

The time), do not do any direct intervention to create better social conditions altho human well-being is a set of socially created phenomena.

Like in the real world (except maybe working with the persons partner or family), and the client/students’ social network don’t get direct participation in the work.

Poor model, harmful for both client and practitioner who are often both chronically strained

One of the things I see missing in consent discourse is ambivalence and mixed feelings. We spend more time in the spaces between yes and no than we do in the decision points, and when we’re in yes and no states there are incredibly nuanced gradations

Our relational language and scaffolding don’t attune us to these gradations in real time even though they’re always occurring. Our framing of consent does not at all match the dynamism of human connection, which is a big part of how we end up with poor results from attempts at it
I’ve got more thoughts on this brewing, that’s it for now. Know that I don’t hold my perspective defensively, I love feedback and curiosity and challenges 😉

As a general rule:

The more nodes of healthy intimacy, the more safe and loving and fun a social fabric will be. Especially when the nodes have dynamism and deep commitment to the fundamentals of good relationshipping.
The problem is we haven’t actually laid out the fundamentals of good relationshipping, and our social structures are stuffy as shit

There’s a need to create healthy soil (a metaphor I think @RichDecibels used recently). That means cultivating ground experiences over peak experiences, that means forgetting high vibrations for a bit and laying down the low end
@RichDecibels Cultivating practices that hone in on the feeling of being restful yet uplifted (which imho is a sense of true naturalness), and doing so both individually and relationally

@RichDecibels I think this is where a lot of the interesting elements of authentic relating/social meditation and other relational modalities can be harnessed, but a lot of those spaces are cultivated with poor sense of core principles, often mirroring the patterns we see in other spaces

@RichDecibels Of relational harm and injury occurring.

This is a lot of the stuff I ponder and try to practice in how I connect these days

I’ll add this: having healthy a relationship with mixed feelings/ambivalence/subtle gradations means that they’re often *pleasant* to be in connection with, as opposed to inducing anxiety. “Not knowing” is actually a place you can enjoy, but we’re weirdly phobic of it culturally https://t.co/zEC77u2HDN

And I’ll add another thing: this isn’t just about sex. The type of dynamic process of connection that unfolds all the time in all of our relationships is laden with opportunities to have all sorts of creativity and dance moves but we play in really confined ways https://t.co/zEC77u2HDN

Good emotional skills here are about simplifying how you dance with relationship

You can do *way* less analysis and just feel into the relational dynamic

Does it feel easeful? Do you what the connection brings out in you? Does receiving their expression feel generally good? https://t.co/QXwe5VMuIh

Just attuning to the felt senses those questions point to is enough; you don’t need to try to analyze or figure out the “why?” (but it’s totally ok if that arises naturally), just allow the natural relational metaphysics to guide how you move in the social field

Insight tends to come easier when you do this, because you’re drawing from the whole relational experience as opposed to just the more pronounced emotional edges and lots of analysis

Key rule here: every layer of analysis you add on top of experience introduces more error

The more points of contact with experience, especially when you’ve tapped into your full range of how you connect with the relational world, the more accurate your insight will be. 

But we tend to be really heady and/or caught up in emotional intoxication/inflammation

What I mean by these:

Emotional intoxication is the sense of escape or high we get when we’re attached to the triggered feeling of positive emotions

Emotional inflammation is how I relate to negative triggers (fight flight freeze fawn etc) where we get stuck with particular…
Parts taking up all of the emotional space

The point at which positivity becomes “toxic” is when “negative” thoughts+feelings are reacted to violently

Trying to stop negative thoughts+feelings will leave you playing psychosomatic whack-a-mole 

Meeting all thoughts+feelings (negative, positive, or mixed) with benevolent, spacious, and caring awareness is the way

In terms of psychosomatic phenomena (thoughts, feelings, spiritual experiences, etc), the truest wisdom applies the same whether the given phenomena is negative/positive/mixed 1/ https://t.co/S4vYg2Q3L4

Really it comes down to an agenda-less benevolence, which 1) allows you to enjoy positive things without grasping, 2) meet negative things with care as opposed to anxious policing, and 3) get cozy with all the myriad complexities across the spectrum without freaking out 2/

The emphasis on awareness here does •not• preclude nimble action, or mean you need to push yourself to endure pain; instead you meet everything with the subtlest and most direct form of compassion, which really boils down to a very natural awareness that supports your 3/

Organic unfolding.

At a certain point the words positive and negative stop making all that much sense; you’ll still be drawn to cultivate positive things but your relationship to all phenomena is fundamentally beyond what those concepts previously pointed to, which 4/

Is really just the natural consequence of allowing yourself to be humbled by this life.

That’s all that good wisdom cultivation really is: allowing yourself to be repeatedly humbled by this life

Any good healer/teacher/mentor/therapist should turn you •towards your life• as opposed to pulling you towards them.

That turn towards may include significant time working with them, but that should always feel like a natural draw, like they don’t need anything from you

I finally made the decision to discontinue my PhD last month, 9.5 years in. My chronic illness remains too debilitating to my level of functioning for that to be realistic (or honestly worthwhile for what I want to do with my time). 1/

I’m really immeasurably proud of the work I’ve done and grateful for the amazing people that were part of the journey (including the challenging ones). I have so many fond memories of teaching, doing therapy, becoming a regular at my favorite coffee shop, finding my voice as a 2/

Therapist, having conflict play out and learning immensely through it, being able to do so in Colorado surrounded by so many other emotional-relational-spiritual explorers. I got to have what felt like a second adolescence where I really figure out who I was, and this illness 3/

In a way set a fire that burned away all the excess such that now I feel more comfortable being who I am and facing literally any challenge life can bring. I am •much• less fuck-with-able now, both on cosmic and human levels 🤭 4/

These last (just under) 4 years have been characterized by grief (I also lost both my parents since getting sick), but that word feels so inadequate for what this all has been. It has been anything but a loss, I know love more deeply than I ever have 5/

I’ve stewarded myself through deep confusion, pain, and crisis; I forgave my father about 6 months before he passed; I got to dote on my mom and return so much of the love she gave to make me into the person I am today 6/

Life delivered me to a place where I have no choice but to live in a way that is fundamentally nourishing bc when I’m not living that way the consequences are astronomical. That’s a tremendous gift and I’m enjoying the hell out of sharing it 7/

I’ll be forever grateful what all I’ve been able to do, most especially my time doing ~1200 clinical hours, 5 years of teaching counseling skills lab courses (3 for undergrad and 3 for 1st year PhD students), and 3.5 years of supervising clinicians in training 8/
I got to see so much magic and have so much time to dedicate to my development and figuring out what it i: to help cultivate emotional spiritual and relational well-being 9/

And now I’m here, more emotionally/ spiritually/relationally well than I’ve ever been. I could say that I wish I could’ve gotten here without the illness and grief, but that seems such a shallow way to relate to it all. It’s more profound yet more simple than words could capture

What I can say is, I don’t have any bitterness, or a sense of being cheated even tho I can’t do many of the things I would if I had a healthy body. My life is immeasurably full, and even the days where I sleep 18 hours from a fatigue crash are beautiful
I’m here for it all, and I know that every moment I get to show up to this life is magic. I’m glad so many of y’all are here to share in it.

Imho the biggest thing missing from many healing modalities is the orientation and capacity to hang out in nourishing states. If you’re not helping develop the basic capacity to find natural resource thru co-regulation/connection, wtf are you doing? Creating cozier disconnection?

When there’s always something to •do• whether it be make it through some emotional release protocol or develop some insight or work towards behavior change, there’s an underlying belief that you or the person you’re supporting •is not ok enough as they are• 1/ https://t.co/GpW4n8S2pg

That doesn’t mean that emotional release, insight, or behavior change aren’t good. But people grow in much more sustainable ways when they have access to the types of low stakes, easily accessible nourishment that comes from organic ok-ness 2/

Maybe calling it “easily accessible” seems like a stretch but with some basic co-regulation skills and scaffolding eg embodied communication and clear+healthy boundaries it’s really easy to teach to clients and their social connections 3/

Most of the basic capacities to get interpersonally resourced are pretty simple and learnable/teachable, but it takes the type of support that nurtures self-awareness and finding your own voice in communicating relationally. Things we should learn growing up but is left out of 4/

Schooling and child-rearing. The very same skills that work for resourcing also work for navigating conflict and complex relational ruptures, but instead we get stuck in processing instead of a down-to-earth connection to •process• 5/

Language are being built during conflict as opposed to during less charged and strained times.

Chronic and debilitating post-viral illness (I’ve spent most of the past ~4 years in bed) has made it a life project to distill the most fundamental levels of nourishment bc strain and non-nourishing states have pretty severe consequences for me 1/ https://t.co/O97Igq7m1Y

I’d already been a meditator about ~8-9 years before I got sick, and years of training in cultivating well-being for self and others through counseling, but I had no idea the levels of nuance I had barely touched, the myriad hooks that kept me in strain and struggle 2/

This isn’t solely personal, obvi the world demands that you contort yourself in some ways to fit in culturally and make yourself a “success.” Even really kind and authentic people are stuck in emotional-relational fields that leave them often under-nourished 3/

But what I continue to find is that there is abundant nourishment when oriented properly. When in connection you can orient to attunement, to finding the just-right kind of connection for the given moment, without pressure to get anywhere specific, spacious abundance arises 4/

There’s a magic to attuning to your system, having the other person/people attune to theirs, and finding the mutually created space that happens when the relational processes of mutual attunement are honored with graceful presence 5/

Most of the substance of connection plays out on the embodied level (even cognitive elements are embodied phenomena, sometimes we’re just so fused to them we can’t attune to the embodied aspects), but we try to establish and maintain intimacy without explicit 6/

Scaffolding for embodied attunement. At best we have mostly cognitive ways of relating where we might dip very briefly into embodied connection+expression. That means there’s an incredible amount of embodied phenomena that’s going unspoken and un-honored, and then we’re left 7/

Wondering how the hell we got to massive ruptures when they’ve been brewing all along. 

I want to be clear that I’m not trying to create an enforced embodiment or that my way of orienting to connection is the one right way, merely that 1) we’re missing out on several 8/

Fundamentals of connection and 2) when we enter into connection we often jump into one way of relating without real consent or choice aside from what people’s typically unconscious patterns are. A big part of this is making relational patterns conscious 9/

The final thing I’ll say is, it might seem really vulnerable to make embodied communication foundational to connection. It is in a way, but when practiced correctly it places low stakes vulnerability first in order to make trust building and attunement transparent whereas 10/

We often build relationships from some moments of attunement and sense of shared interests but don’t make attunement practices a central piece, finding brittleness later when connections aren’t built consciously

It’s been an extremely tedious journey these last few years but I have irl and online community where I can drop into organic connection with people and we can puzzle into mutually nourishing ways of playing. It’s the best.

Reciprocal embodied communication is by far the most efficient way to find the just-right moment-to-moment connection. I can’t tell you how rad it is to feel and stay with the feeling when eg someone shares an embodied shift then my whole spine relaxes (prob by the function 1/ https://t.co/eQUXpN3haL

Of mirror neurons etc). Imho we have a v limited idea of what heart to heart connection is like when it’s purely story driven (not that that’s bad), like we only get there through sharing vulnerable •content• when it’s available in many other forms and fashions 2/

Essentially what I like to do is create playing fields that welcome all the things we already do (including things like story when its right) but prioritize embodied attunement first so each person tracks their own experience and the relational field moves together organically 3/

In order to do this responsibly, I’m dedicated to orienting people not towards me as some some type of “guru” but instead teaching methods as a non-hierarchical practice to take into their chosen families/social webs to create the type of relationships that will care for them 4/

Across the lifespan (and doing the same for myself on a personal level so I’m not getting my needs met through work).

This practice is generally very simple and designed to help people including myself find their own natural voice and the people it resonates with most deeply 5/

@annafordable Particular forms of social meditation and embodied authentic relating. I spent many years practicing along side my grad school work and meditation practice and really just try to distill it to the most basic fundamentals of connection

@annafordable Eg I usually start with the noticing game, one person starts “Being in connection with you I feel ____” just naming relationally salient feelings and sensations without story/explanation, then the other person goes “Hearing that I feel ____,” going back and forth and witnessing

@annafordable The unfolding of the relational process. (It’s ok if story/interpretation comes in some; the main thing is to primarily attend to one’s own embodied experience and avoid questions and talking about the other person’s experience as much as possible for clear boundaries’ sake)

@annafordable Then after the basics of just speaking from emotions/sensations is established, two additional layers can be added 1) offers and invitations to make relational moves (eg, I feel warm and connected, would you be up to hold hands?) and 2) story and interpretation (bringing it in

@annafordable Trust, then direct people inward (“Being in connection with myself I notice…” with me responding as a support person). 

Once they’ve established a sense of their own emotional journey I encourage them to bring in a loved one (partner, friend, family member, collaborator) and

@annafordable Help them to learn how to do it together, very much encouraging creativity. It’s not about loyalty to a format but instead what creates an organic relational field that invites authentic, sustainable mutual support

@annafordable A lot of what I’m doing personally right now is finding other people that I vibe with (whether more friendly or more collaborator-level) and practicing together and building mutual support before looking to build anything beyond that

@annafordable My going theory at the moment is if you till the soil with good nutrients and fine tuned attention, lots of healthy stuff will grow.

But we tend to build quickly, instead trying to fit things to structures (that we’re not always aware we’ve internalized) as opposed to allowing

@annafordable them to grow naturally.

Imo this post + the one it qt’d are flawed, but I will say that the current model and way people are trained leaves psychotherapy broadly as a sort emotional bureaucracy, putting things nicely into their little compartments but leaving foundational issues mostly untouched 1/ https://t.co/dOkODbjegg

I have a ton of thoughts on this, how it leaves therapists susceptible to burnout, what exactly about the model makes it this way, etc etc. Some of biggest things are individualization + compartmentalization of healing, disconnected language and opaque relational scripts/power 2/

Negotiations, minimal direct connection to building mutually nurturing social fabrics, and a lack of a clear sense of what it is to cultivate and take in embodied nourishment in real time.

Could obviously spend a lot of time unpacking this, but what factors make it so someone 3/

No longer needs therapy? That’s almost entirely relational. Yes, the way therapy works •can• help a person’s relationship with themselves and with others to an extent, but therapy doesn’t directly build networks, maaaaybe teaches skills that could support it 4/

Yeah this is an important point. Vulnerability is •one component• of developing an emotionally healthy life, and learning how to calibrate it and titrate it properly can take a lot of work, especially bc many of our models prioritize riskier forms of vulnerability 1/ https://t.co/9M8AEqw0Qh

A common phenomenon in the therapy/healing/spiritual world is having a “vulnerability hangover.” This is usually because our scripts for vulnerability focus on one sided expression as opposed to mutual attunement practices, and these scripts also focus on story and catharsis

Story isn’t bad, but it’s a process that’s layered with many relational and internal moves that are often opaque in the dynamics they’re shared within, along with the fact that stories can be wielded against people.

Catharsis can be just fine, but it can also be a collective process through which the person expressing it just happens to be a release valve and if the dynamics haven’t been transparent enough they often arrive there through an unclear consent process

Vulnerability is a big part of collective growth that’s being called for imho, but it really has to be distilled to the fundamentals of how it’s done safely, with people who are in mutually supportive connections, where power can be navigated transparently

Yeah, people can end up trying to police goals out of their experience when they hear these things from poor teachers or delivery systems, but truly having no goal means also letting go of the goal of having no goals, ie naturalness and healthy approach is in the paradox https://t.co/gIjF7PPIAB

I really enjoy particular forms of authentic relating/social meditation and think they can help open up relational scripts that are stilted + lack choice, but the way these events/scenes get organized and scaffolded doesn’t translate into helping people have robust social webs 1/ https://t.co/l3Df4XKQvE
While the practices might be radically transformative in some ways, the without a radical orientation toward building real connection, they’re missing much of the most transformative potential 2/

The small talk discourse really isn’t about whether small talk is good or bad imho, it’s about choice and consent and creativity in connection. Small talk is just fine but there’s a bunch of opaque relational processes happening underneath that rarely get spoken to 3/

The types of intimacy that authentic relating and cuddle parties etc can create open people up to possibilities they often haven’t  experienced before, and if they’re not your thing that’s cool, and there are certainly spaces/scenes that practice them in unsafe ways 4/

What I’m interested in is turning myself towards my own social web and nurturing healthy and authentic intimacy from the ground up + inside out, and helping others do the same. If you distill the right pieces from AR and cuddle spaces and intimacy games… 5/

There’s whole fields of exploration and nourishment to be had. Just a bit of humility and willingness to be patient in finding the right people to build connection with will get you going in the right direction ime.

It’s a mistake imho that we associate coming to cognitive insights (eg about our stories/histories, our beliefs) that produce states of relief as the primary source of healing (though they are supportive). The foundation is orienting to sustainable, embodied nourishment 1/

Our language conventions aren’t built for nourishment and aren’t the most direct pathway. A focus on your nervous system and it’s relationship to the shared nervous system of your social web, esp your chosen family/community/close collaborators, is much more direct and 2/
More likely to lead to sustained well-being because it’s not solely individually focused

When your nervous system is resourced and you can orient to embodied generativity/nourishment, 1) you have more pathways to well-being than our traditional language scripts allow and 2) the cognitive insights come more easily because more of your whole experience is involved

Cognitively based methods can give you states of well-being that can 
 last for significant periods, but without the fundamentals of nervous system-focused skills you’re not going to have all of your perceptual tools at your disposal for navigating an emotional life https://t.co/zW4u3jGvfw

You can’t castigate the world into being a more generative and healthy place.

You have to seize upon the generative and healthy elements and nurture them so the harmful elements stop getting used to get needs met

Even when you have really complementary emotional intelligences and aligned beliefs/views of your relationship with another person, the primary method by which the relationship is sustained is nervous system attunement

Enjoy parallel play with a person? Your nervous systems are well-attuned. Enjoy thought provoking conversations with a person? Your nervous systems are well-attuned. Enjoy sex? Boy, you know your nervous systems are well-attuned. Enjoy sitting in silence together? Play wrestling?

A common mistake is to try to police your discomfort instead of orienting to nourishment

This is the difference between hyper-vigilance and grace. I’ve seen seasoned meditators and people with years of therapy who are still just policing discomfort. An orientation to nourishment sets a foundation instead of being reactive. 1/ https://t.co/NOQcnkukCS

It also turns out grace arises naturally as you practice with nourishment over time; effort gets subtler + subtler until you’re not efforting at all, finding that naturalness is the most efficient pathway to emotional well-being (naturalness doesn’t preclude action, to be clear)

Rage is fine and welcome, no better or worse than any other emotion. But it needs to be met with grace so that if you act on it you’re acting from emotional sobriety. Otherwise it’s easy to coerce/harm yourself or others with your rage while convinced you’re acting righteously 1/
RT @WoohkaStylie: That most people operate an inner police state gives us a lot of insight into how society and culture get organized.
If you can’t get to a place of emotional sobriety, it’s a matter of finding the supports that nurture you. Rage points to needs for safety, connection, nurturance 2/

If you’re in escalating conflict, the skill is to know your embodiment well enough to respond to rage/anger to de-escalate inflammation. Even if there are legit issues between people, you can’t sort those above a certain level of escalation where people only hurt self/other 3/

When orienting to friendships/relationships from a place of possession + collection, the natural ebbs and flows of intimacies feel threatening. When we cultivate fields of connection with an orientation to dynamism + generativity, ebbs and flows feel like creative opportunities

Cults, cult-like dynamics, and manipulative/coercive relational patterns are characterized by possession + collection (and the extraction that goes along with it). There’s a lot of subtlety and skillfulness that goes into building commitment w/o possession/collection https://t.co/44nV2cWKhn

While there’s been systematic organization of relationships for purposes of survival and production, there’s whole creative realms of intimacies that have been barely explored cuz our relational scripts and structures are stuffy to the point of being claustrophobic

We’re having these layered embodied experiences but instead of directly tapping into them for their nourishing and generative potentials we relate either cognitively or through consumption or parallel play or inherited intimacy scripts (which are fine but limited) https://t.co/MSVlwshihN

Current theory based on my experience and process:

There’s layers of loyalty and devotion to naturalness, nourishment, and generativity where, just with appropriately subtle attunement,  relational processes become entirely emergent. 1/

True nourishment and generativity are naturally reinforcing; surrendering skillfully to your humanity lets that process come fully online, whereas straining to control not only makes it feel like “work” but also impedes your relational dexterity 2/

What I mean by “relational dexterity” is the fine motor skills of connection, the micro moves that are the mark of maturity and embodiment in connection. Collectively we’re still at very low levels of refined relational skills 3/

Devotion to naturalness is important in part because it keeps you from building relationships from a place of strain. Stretching+straining to form+maintain relationships is how you end up injured due to unhealthy foundations 4/

Nourishment seems common sense/intuitive but we’re in a time of deep intoxication and dissociation. True nourishment is both grounded and uplifted, genuinely wholesome (which isn’t as twee as you would think), and it makes intoxication/escape less appealing 5/

Generativity is the basic sense that growth is happening in connection. Human life is fundamentally dynamic and if there isn’t a felt sense of relating more and more subtly to that dynamism, then stagnancy can really suffocate one’s spirits 6/

Why I from this as a practice of loyalty and devotion is that, we’re used to thinking of loyalty and devotion to our concepts of what our relationships should look like. In practice that’s fairly ham-fisted. 7/

In order to be fully devoted to any single relationship, you have to be deeply connected to the full social-relational field surrounding that relationship. Cognitive models and rule sets simply aren’t connected or embodied enough tools to reliably support that practice
This is one of my favorite takes https://t.co/UIdxWUea5w

Naturalness, nourishment, + generativity as foundational orientations, as practices of loyalty and devotion, set a foundational skillfulness that allows you to gravitate towards the unfolding field of connection like you’re being carried by the natural winds and currents of life

This, however, doesn’t at all mean you’re disengaged. You lose the image you had of what you thought it meant to be engaged with life and find that deep engagement emerges naturally. What could be called divine will is generally really mundane
That deep engagement that comes with healthy forms of surrender unlocks so much in terms of patience, in terms of intuitive relational skills, in terms of sustainable discipline.
