I’ve talked abt emotional/relational sobriety before; it’s also really important for cultivating networks that are robust against manipulative behavior. I’ll try to explain why 1/

Ppl who are stuck in manipulative patterns of relating to others are stuck operating in modes of control, intoxication, or dissociation. Emotional/relational sobriety, or a state of grounded easeful and spacious connection with self+others, is difficult for manipulators bc 2/

Then they are left feeling the pain and instability they’ve contorted themselves around and tried to escape from for much of their lives. So, immediately if you’re cultivating relationships from embodied sobriety manipulators will either be bored and not drawn to you or make 3/

themselves apparent much sooner. Ppl who are chronic manipulators can create wonderful things but stable emotional well-being is not one of them except perhaps when they are held by an extended network that is incredibly robust against manipulation but in service of support 4/

To be robust against manipulation, it truly has to be a collective effort, bc manipulators are •proactively• making chess moves to be wielded emotionally and relationally, and if you’re trying to logic through it or trust ppl to see it, well, manipulators make it so things 5/

Aren’t clear because they can’t allow themselves to be seen, including to themselves. They will create battle lines, and by the time those are drawn it can be hard not to lose relationships or reputations etc bc our current sociocultural frameworks make manipulation opaque 6/
This means, to me, from a lot of personal and professional experience with manipulators, that making networks robust to this type of behavior requires a bottom up process. 7/

I believe it takes orienting to individual, one-on-one, and small group low stakes practices of emotional sobriety to make all the individual nodes and each connection in the web one that is 1) generally nourished and resourced + 2) able to perceive when someone is trying to  8/

If you find yourself in a family or social group/network with a high level of conflict, there’s likely at least one manipulator in the field, and a lot of people who are susceptible to being enablers/codependents for the manipulator(s) 1/ https://t.co/PfFvR7bEl0

There might be a central conflict with ppl taking different sides. In my experience there is generally usually one manipulator driving the systematic drama because manipulators aren’t good at feeding each other’s supply. That doesn’t mean that others don’t engage in instances 2/

Of manipulation/harm, but there are people who have chronic patterns of manipulative behavior that don’t develop insight or find capacity to compassionately understand their impact. Bc of this, people enmeshed with them might be pushed to escalated behavior bc normal boundary 3/

Setting does not work. There are things to be on the look out for: 1) casual meanness/condescension 2) speaking for other people to get you on their side 3) small to big lies 4) inability to respond to critique without defensiveness (defensiveness meaning the inability to 4/

Consider that they could be wrong or have caused harm. That’s different from a person who welcomes feedback but gives their pov in good faith) 5) any perceived wrong against them is a big injustice but their wrongs are moved past quickly 6) they say all the right things when 5/

Called out but behavior doesn’t change or even gets worse.

Chronic manipulators don’t stop when a particular relationship ends, the energy just shifts or goes to new sources of supply. So if you really want to support the shifting of a high conflict environment, you have to 6/

Figure out where the locuses of chronic conflict are, set boundaries between those people, and see where manipulation still bubbles up. Honestly once you know these things well it’s not hard to see whose doing what, but usually people in the extended network are part of the 7/

Chronic manipulator’s extended defenses, so you have to go slowly and develop robustly wholesome and high integrity relationships amongst as many people in the network as possible so low integrity bullshit gets flushed out 8/

Exert power. This is also why non-hierarchical relational practices are important; not to say all hierarchy is bad, but to have a deep sense of sober non-hierarchy before choosing how to navigate power as you take on higher stakes projects. 9/

The work of emotional-relational soil regeneration imho is cultivating low stakes connection practices and creating networks within which the processes and terms/conditions of connection can become explicit. The fact that the process and terms/conditions are usually opaque is 1/

Part of the biggest problems: people don’t realize they’re not in alignment/attunement until way far down the line because they’re tracking mental models and ideas of connection as opposed to the actual process and embodied sense of connection. 2/

This is also what makes people/groups/networks/institutions susceptible to manipulators and bad actors: we’re listening for what people portray as opposed to the underlying truth that is revealed in process-oriented, emotionally grounded and sober connection practices 3/

(When I refer to emotional sobriety I don’t mean that intoxicating emotions need to be policed; more so that the connection practices are oriented to a foundation of restful, agendaless connection - from which intoxicating states of connection are welcome if they’re organic) 4/
(Also I’m still just beginning to flesh out this soil regeneration metaphor so feel free to riff off this or ask questions) 5/

The soil regeneration piece feels important cuz there’s something intuitive to the feeling of a social field that is well aerated and healthy vs stagnant and riddled with unwanted toxicity, and the mission of doing so calls for an unselfish orientation: what invites ppl into 1/ https://t.co/thzjHsi8DN

The right ecologies/environments for their personal connections, career/life projects, and adventuring. I think these processes can be distilled so much more efficiently and effectively as compared to the rudimentary tools at our disposal now 2/

I practice particular types of embodied social meditation because it works like tuning an instrument (I know, I’m mixing metaphors). When I drop in for just like 5-10 minutes with a friend, then the ensuing conversation moves with grace and flow because we actively tuned into 3/

The connection. I think integrating this type of practice in low stakes ways can support healthy relational ecologies, working from an inside-out and bottom-up framework. Starting with small amounts in smaller units of connection, working up to deeper explorations 4/
There’ll be more for me to say but that’s it for now

A thread:

A lot of our emotional norms center around story, interpretation, memory, and anticipation. These things are fine, but they’re limited compared to real time embodied connection + awareness when it comes to actual nourishment 1/ https://t.co/dI5AuPBDEy

Eg, it’s less important to be able to create gratitude lists than it is to be able to feel the substance of gratitude when it arises naturally and actually allow yourself to receive + enjoy it on your own terms. 2/
There’s all sorts of psycho-somatic emotional phenomena going on in our connections but our language and social scripts fairly actively dissociate us from this reality. 3/

This contorts our emotional experiences, such that we often only tap into them directly when they’re intense. But there’s •so• much nourishment to be had in low risk/low intensity emotional connection, to touch and touched with friends/partners/collaborators/etc 4/
And tracking the real time process of connection, with clear senses of emotional boundaries, also translates into much healthier conflict skills. It’s really the underlying process of attunement 5/

I will say, my chronic illness has heavily influenced me in this direction, in part bc it becomes so clear how much energy and strain goes into shaping ourselves into conventional relational norms, and also bc I’ve needed the emotional connection to find sustained nourishment 6/

Most emotional well-being isn’t particularly about being “seen” or understood, it’s about being connected and touched. If you’re consistently connected and emotionally touched/held, insight and understanding typically arrive organically. 7/

The fundamentals for all of this are fairly simple and can be learned (maybe best learned) in low stakes practice. When you scaffold conversation/connection skillfully, co-regulation and connection can arise in a way that makes room for the just right level of intimacy 8/
All of what I’m talking about does include some risk, but no more than everyday relating processes; literally all ways of connecting can be harnessed for manipulative or harmful purposes.  9/

From my pov this is a level of emotional-relational work emerging from a vast field of people trying different ways of nourishing human connection. And from what I’ve seen personally and professionally, we really need it. End/

Imho a fundamental question for a healthy relationship with pleasure:

Are you extracting pleasure or are you engaging pleasure?

Extraction is transactional, engagement is relational. Extraction depletes resources; engagement is resource neutral to positive

This is closely related to the distinction between states of nourishment and states of intoxication. Extraction and intoxication aren’t inherently harmful, but those edges need to be met responsibly in order for them to be nourishing https://t.co/9xs67mS0dP

Primary skill for well-being:

Fine-tuned awareness to the point at which exertion becomes strain, and ability to recalibrate to healthy exertion or rest nimbly
brb getting nimble as fuck https://t.co/FvoAm96PFZ

The fastest pathway to well-being I’ve found is growing a network of organically, patiently authentic friendships. It only took my entire nervous system going to hell from chronic illness to make any contrivance or strain intolerable for me to get really good at this 😭

I honestly don’t think this would’ve been possible if I didn’t have somatic social meditation practices to center attunement and nourishment, cuz typical language and social scripts include so many layers of processing and psycho-somatic contortion 1/ https://t.co/T3GgJcPsdX

Co-regulation is always happening, ie our nervous systems are always impacting each other, and there’s fairly simple ways to harness that in service of grounded, clean senses of well-being. 

It’s like drinking clean water or breathing fresh air when you really sync up 2/

My general sense is simply that, from years of study and practice and being obliterating-ly humbled by some life challenges, our social practices can be much more effective in terms of generating easeful, low stakes nourishment. 3/

This all comes down to fundamentals; in many ways I had my body and spirit breakdown, and reviving it from the ground up has been a process of discerning the basics.

Rn I think of them as the grounded/spacious forms of nourishment, naturalness, and generativity 4/

Each of these has a somatic signature

Nourishment - simply the felt sense of being resourced/uplifted in real-time
Naturalness - the sense that your body-mind-spirit are acting freely, without contrivance
Generativity - the sense that growth/dynamism are organically engaged 5/

That can feel abstract to connect to, but really its just an intentional re-orientation away from less wholesome ways of engaging that we may be stuck in

Naturalness, nourishment, and generativity are all naturally reinforcing, so you don’t have to •seek• them, just listen 6/

When you simply listen to your body, to your emotional centers, you’ll start to perceive where the grounded, organic wellness shows up (or is at least possible). Gravitating to the right people happens pretty naturally when our senses are well-tuned to the relational field /end

Really just think we need to distill the very foundations of what regenerative social connection feels and looks like, and to do so in ways that orient people to chosen family, village, and community as opposed to orienting to institutions/businesses/individual leaders/clubs

Ok feeling a little spicy and got a thread on what I perceive to be a big limitation of psychotherapy as a model (applies to much coaching as well). Your thoughts are welcome (but I won’t spend much energy in argumentative stuff, I’m tired) 1/

Big limitation of psychotherapy is that it’s not a regenerative model of connection. You extract support through transaction. Therapists end up burnt out, clients get confused dealing with the relational opacity of the clinical framework and v limited practice w mutual support 2/

Regenerative models create networks of connection and are built upon mutual support as opposed to one-way empathy. It’s not to say therapy is bad, just that we haven’t fleshed out perhaps the most important part of the model of emotional-relational health. 3/

Psychotherapy is helpful for psycho-emotional-relational-spiritual injuries and chronic conditions. But imho it’s a poor model for directly cultivating the networks people need to be consistently emotionally-relationally nourished 4/

Embodied connection + mutual support feels like play, dancing, wrestling, cuddling, touching, resting, even if you’re not making physical contact, so much of what’s vibrant about human life, + can be learned in low stakes/low risk ways that can build to deeper practice 5/

But psychotherapy almost never makes room for direct practice of these things, let alone directly engaging someone’s relational field to build the friendships/chosen family/village/community people need to be well fed consistently. PEOPLE NEED A VILLAGE DAMN 6/

There’s a large amount of psychosomatic contortion that goes into the stance of psychotherapist, which is related to professional class norms. These knots are an integral thing to unpack if we want a healthy culture, but current norms place therapists in a difficult position 7/

I think this is also part of what makes people susceptible to spiritual charlatans and cult leaders: so much of the magic of humanity is squeezed out of people through our institutions that by the time they’re trained as therapists a lot of the work is flat. 8/

Manipulators + cult leaders can learn a few emotional-relational-spiritual parlor tricks and get people high in order to manipulate them, and the type of language and embodiment the rest of culture offers doesn’t have enough transparent pathways to healthy human magic 9/

I think the current moment is calling for sociocultural roles that are structured differently, that have greater capacity to help people directly tap into their relational fields and harness unpolluted emotional-relational nourishment. 10/

There’s •so much• emotional-relational pollution, and our communication is over-processed. We’re primarily cognitive, and we’re filtering our selves and how we communicate through layers of abstraction and projection and performance, there’s v little un-processed connection 11/

So our interpersonal diet is made up of highly processed foods, there’s so much parallel consumption as intimacy and extractive pleasure practice (both extracting from self and from other), many people don’t even know what resting in easeful embodied connection feels like 12/

The most efficient way to get there imho is through direct communication of the process of co-regulation + the ebbs and flows of intimacy. Ever since my body broke down with illness, this has been my central practice 13/

I spent 7 years in deep study and practice at a fairly elite counseling psych PhD before I got too sick to continue, and even before I was sick it was clear: not only was the field not creating cultures of nourishment, it could barely create nourishment for individuals 14/

Consistent nourishment is the product of a symbiotic and dynamic relationship to the relational ecology one is embedded within. But people are rarely touching and being touched emotionally-relationally, they’re mostly talking about (processing and consuming) their lives 15/

Psychotherapy has real value and will continue to, but there’s some elements clearly missing, and much like so many other institutions and fields at the moment, it’s looking like there’s some fundamental re-imaginings that need to happen 16/

One of the most important honesties I’ve had to practice in this young-ish life

Welcoming the possibility that love is absent or too polluted to access cleanly in places and relationships where I had assumed it was present 1/

The thing is, welcoming •fully• the possibility of lack often revealed just how much profound love and connection there was (even if there was harm in the dynamic). But I had to stop hiding from the fear of what its absence could mean and let it be a possibility 2/

Love doesn’t live in interpretation or image; it lives in experience and presence

In most cases it was not so much that love was absent; sometimes people •have• love but are poor practitioners of it. Never pretend that a poor lover is a source of wealth. 3/

Just a new symptom due to the sedentary-ness this illness enforces, or am I dying? It seems extreme but there are points where mortality 2/

makes itself known and must be met like a friend as opposed to anxiety/control/dismissal

When working with anger, there are initial challenges that typically look like welcoming your own justified anger + finding healthy self-compassion for the reasons people could be justifiably angry with you. Difficult processes but fairly straightforward 1/ https://t.co/pf8YQmIgeW

What’s left is a process that will bring you into direct contact with contradictions, of your own and of others and of the world and of how morality + justice are framed. You could spend your entire life trying to find the right side of something that is actually pointing to 2/

The levels at which life doesn’t have “sides.” Or you can allow yourself to be humbled+confused, and spend your time puzzling towards right action. Anger isn’t primarily about expression or “letting it out,” tho it can involve those things 3/

Anger is a call to harness the preciousness of this life and the human spirit in service of the types of compassion and justice that can’t be captured by concept or dogma. True wisdom here emerges in part when you recognize that you can never fully eliminate your own ignorance 4/

You can only develop the humility to know that your ideas + beliefs + analyses are always enormously limited, and given that limitation -> what can be undertaken that welcomes grace for the fact that ignorance is an organic phenomenon, endemic to all of humanity? 5/

I can’t pretend to know what the right formation of grace is for any given person or relationship or group or sociopolitical issue. What I can do is puzzle towards creating the conditions from which it arises, having witnessed the everyday magic of people 6/

Who have harnessed it (which is literally everyone once you can see its myriad manifestations). What I can say is that, listening, attunement, and willingness to take appropriate/grounded risks all seem helpful. /end

Only a small % of attunement is conceptual, which is why “processing” is an inefficient tool to personal or relational well-being. It’s like noticing your guitar is out of tune, then spending your time talking about what it would like for it to be in-tune and then concluding 1/
That the good feelings that arise from saying the “right” things about your hypothetical in-tune guitar is the same as actually being in-tune. Meanwhile no tuning has happened. 2/

It’s much more about being in compassionate connection with the unfolding embodied •process,• which is more likely to be in-touch with reality as opposed to facsimiles of reality that can be created when we reify thought as truth through processing 3/

Process in this case being the embodied, holistic sense of what is unfolding in real time. “Processing” = stories + interpretations. Process = present moment emotions, sensations, and relational interplay 4/
Process can include thought, but it’s not primarily centered there, recognizing that truth can never be captured conceptually, only pointed to (at best) 5/

RT @KennethFolk: We can hold each other accountable without rancor. This is aspirational, but it matters to me.

I need your help finding some word(s). I’ve been thinking about the process through which people find their way towards •their people• (eg chosen family, village), and I’ve been thinking along the lines of the notion of relational “triage” 1/

That is, the process of feeling into connections and growing towards some/away from others, deepening and prioritizing with more and more dexterity as individuals and the group field become more skillful. But triage feels perhaps too clinical 2/

So I have been thinking about ecological metaphors, eg my next offering is rooted in “seed pods” that welcome the germination/incubation of deeper connection while also allowing the organic process of dispersion or “failure” to germinate (not a bad thing) 3/
But I think I need a word that isn’t “triage” to describe the process of sifting and sorting through connections to find one’s alignment with the right people. 4/

So if other words pop up, or if you any thoughts or questions come up related to the thoughts shared here, I’d love to hear what you think! 

End of thread 😌

Healthy relationshipping for me lately has been about surrendering agenda and finding what connection arises from naturalness. I don’t have to try to be confident; I just trust the ground beneath my feet, and when the ground isn’t clear that’s not a connection to pursue atm 1/

I’ve certainly done a lot of skill development but now it seems the conversations that need be had (eg about boundaries, relationship expectations, etc) arise naturally when the connection needs to find more confident/nuanced footing 2/

Treating mixed feelings/ambivalence as a welcome friend is one of •the• most important skills for a healthy and happy life and relationships. Our closest relationships (with self, with others) are always going to have various flavors of mixed feelings 1/
Think about it, how often do you have a singular, pure version of a feeling, unaffected by any other? 2/

We get into trouble when we get stuck in reactivity to ambivalence/mixed feelings, often getting anxiety/panic and scrambling to “figure it out.” Making friends with ambivalence allows you to be comfortable even on shaky ground and find discernment as you move forward 3/

@eatnik @selflathing Often narcissists care a lot but have incredible difficulty communicating it directly and clearly due to how their sense of self interplays with their interpersonal environment. @eatnik @selflathing The types of relational behavior that drives people away from narcissists are almost always the result of their own severe mistreatment/neglect/trauma (which is why there’s a movement to relabel many PDs as C-PTSD, or at least integrate that awareness more fully to lower stigma)

@eatnik @selflathing It’s simply hard for a person within the set of conditions they’ve been subjected to to have a strong ability to have a stable sense of self, so they care deeply but NPD is in large part an interpersonal disorder that shapes their behavior in difficult ways

@eatnik @selflathing Yeah, I also wonder about what type of treatment models could actually reach people with NPD (they rarely get treatment). A lot of the ways people with NPD display manipulative behavior mirrors the way compulsively agreeable/co-dependent people manipulate themselves, so I think

@eatnik @selflathing It would take a fairly immersive model because someone with NPD typically reads ambiguous social signals as potentially threatening (existence itself is an ambiguous signal that’s interpreted as a threat they defend by inflating their sense of sense in various ways)

Imho this is what unconditional love is: when you let go of contrivance, performance, or trying to maintain/construct an idea of self/other/the connection, what’s left?

Imo people need lots of low stakes yet nourishing reps of emotional-relational fundamentals distributed across a network of connections much more than people need catharsis or processing. The fundamentals •lead• to healthy catharsis and processing, but there’s no guarantee 1/

That processing or catharsis lead to the fundamentals of a healthy life. Too much Western healing and spiritual work is compartmentalized and individualized to the point that even trying to focus on the fundamentals is limited to one shared language with a small number of 2/

People that aren’t integrated into your life. Finding your voice, cultivating consistent nourishment and relative safety, these are things that are most effectively and efficiently accomplished through attunement to a whole field of connection. Sustainable well-being requires 3/

A healthy distribution of connection across many relationships (even if you’re v introverted/independent you’re still reliant on a large network of people around you), and this simply isn’t effectively facilitated by individualized and compartmentalized models of healing 4/

When I say compartmentalized here I mean it literally, most attempts at healing are taking place in little compartments (therapists’ offices, retreats, etc) removed from your life that the people integral to your life only get second hand glimpses of, or you’re trying to do 5/

Self-help by reading and doing exercises like it’s school and you’re learning the “right” skills as opposed to the collaborative + emergent skills that arise when there’s mutual attunement between people 6/

I see it all the time between people who have done lots of personal work: they’ve put in tons of •individualized• effort so when they get in conflict they throw over-developed “skills” at each other as opposed to knowing how to orient to ground of mutual emergence 7/

The healthiest relationships (platonic, romantic, collaborative, etc) I’ve observed are amongst those people who are committed to being comfortable with their own and each other’s discomfort to the point that what could be called “conflict” isn’t actually that uncomfortable.

1/ https://t.co/yp6ExbGsVv

The healthy alternative to “conflict avoidance” isn’t having a lot of difficult conversations or “processing” your feelings (which is usually processing thoughts and interpretations, not feelings).

2/

The healthy alternative is non-manipulation, both of self and other. Inner and outer senses of conflict are signals that point to where integrity lives in each moment. Sometimes integrity is just acknowledging uncertainty where previously there was certainty.

3/

When you stop trying to make yourself or others different than they are, when you stop arguing with the past or contorting to a vision of the future you’re grasping for, when you stop trying to make the present different, integrity with the available truth is all that’s left 4/

Then either what would’ve been “conflict” just becomes acknowledgement of truth and/or the conversation that ensues doesn’t add more pain than is naturally present sorting through the field to find the pathway(s) of integrity  5/

Just to clarify this point, non-manipulation of others does •not• preclude you from naming an objection to harmful behavior. But it does mean you’re attuned to when that’s useful for a situation vs finding other pathways that are more agentic/useful 1/ https://t.co/X4al7ebNdE
Sometimes all we know in the face of something personally or collectively unjust is intoxication on anger and wielding it like a weapon, either against others or against ourselves or both 2/
Non-manipulation is simply an acknowledgement of things as they are and a living breathing embodied curiosity towards the path of creative integrity 3/

When the only certain thing is “There’s a sense of uncertainty when I think about this person” or “I feel afraid” or “I feel intoxicatingly high thinking about the possibility here.” 2/

The stories aren’t bad, but they should be part of a balanced diet where the majority of what you’re oriented to is •unprocessed, whole experience,• and the more narrativizing I do, the more I’m chopping up and processing what was whole into something contrived 3/

The mark of good stories is that they point efficiently and effectively back to whole grain truth. A poor story is one that obscures the truth (eg trying to convince myself to feel good towards a person when the truth is there is mostly pain there) 4/
Imho becoming skillfully oriented to whole, unprocessed experience is the most effective way to 1) enjoy life as it is and 2) guide oneself to holistically healthy outcomes 5/

The relational practice I come back to is an embodied curiosity towards “Where does mutual invitation live?” So often we get caught up trying to grasp for what we think we need to be happy in connection, or preventing what we think would destroy us, that we get tunnel vision 1/ https://t.co/KZjHBYM7Or

“Potential outcome I’d enjoy.” It’s ok if a worry about a harmful outcome arrives, but I treat that as an opportunity to orient towards where healthful connection is available moment to moment as opposed to grasping for more control than is available (eg, rumination) 4/

At its root tending to your connections is just noticing where mutual nourishment is available, practicing a healthy distribution of energy across nourishing connections so neither yourself nor your connections are either in danger of drowning or going malnourished 5/

To be able to spot manipulation, you have to be aware of how you manipulate yourself. There’s a place of naturalness from which it becomes easy to tell if you’re being pulled on by someone else, but if you’re unaware of how you start contorting yourself, it’s easy to miss it.

This was something I noticed in myself over the course of some personal reckoning. If I felt attracted to someone, I’d notice parts of me that started to try to fit what I think they’d like almost immediately. 1/ https://t.co/1CXy2x4iSY

Many of us do this, and it’s part of what makes us susceptible to manipulation. If you’re used to neurotically shaping yourself to be a “good” partner/student/friend/son or daughter/etc, it’s easier for others to mold you. 2/

If you find the place of naturalness, it’s easier to find the relational contexts where the best of you is drawn out •without effort•

People stuck in manipulative patterns (manipulating self and/or others) typically have a hard time with naturalness bc they’re avoiding pain 3/

To avoid that pain, they’re in chronic patterns of control, performance, production, intoxication, and/or dissociation, without the space for easeful sober naturalness bc they haven’t had the right environmental support to safely get there 4/

It’s not a guarantee (all humans are capable of manipulation of self and others), but ability to access naturalness in connection is a green flag for relational health, and personally it’s the one I prioritize most, not from a place of pressure but from a place of cultivation 5/

Sometimes the pursuit of depth is the avoidance of sober naturalness, from which healthy depth comes organically.

Healthy loyalty isn’t about shaping yourself to fit agreements and roles. It’s about being humbly dedicated to mutual health + well-being and being honest with self + other(s) through the cycles of growth and decay that happen in all connections

Really any identity is susceptible to this sort of thing, it’s just obvious in spirituality right now because spirituality is having a Moment™️ and there’s a lot of contrivance+grifting out there

Imho orientation to identity is a process of self-objectification 1/ https://t.co/C77TQh8KzL

This can manifest in ways we traditionally think of as objectification, ie shaping oneself to fit a mold of what we think looks like a “good” spiritual person, or parent, or man/woman/non-binary person, etc etc (whichever kind of “good” we’ve attached our worth to) 2/

It can also manifest in forms of collection or materialism, where we neurotically collect people (friends, collaborators, partners, children), experiences (peak experiences, achievements, memories), or views/beliefs as objects that we attach our precarious sense of worth to 3/

When we attach a precarious sense of worth to views/beliefs, relationships, experiences, or self-presentation, then the natural impermanence and ambiguity and shape shifting of all of these things becomes threatening and needs to be neurotically controlled 4/

Imho this is a very common dis-ease of the moment, and I’ve certainly had and continue to have my own experiences with it. Trying to police it can easily become counter-productive and neurotic in and of itself 5/

The healthy re-calibration here, as far as I can tell, is simply to sober naturalness. When you let go of grasping and also let go of policing, the parts that are contrived and/or aren’t serving the well-being of your self and loved ones crumble away naturally 6/

Note: Sobriety here doesn’t mean you’re neurotically policing out intoxicating feelings or experiences, simply that you’re oriented to pathways of restful upliftedness where intoxicating experience can be approached in healthy and sustainable ways 7/

Oo couple people found this and reminded me of it. Let’s talk needs :)

Imho orienting to the foundations of emotional-relational healthfulness is countercultural in a world so shaped into production, performance, competition, and consumption 1/ https://t.co/vl4uLqyq2c

None of those things are bad in + of themselves, but it’s obvious they’re imbalanced in the current moment. So we end up demanding particular things (feelings, expressions, performances, etc) from ourselves and/or others cuz that’s the language we know, or we dissociate 2/
(Note: I also don’t think dissociation is inherently bad; it’s a natural process and is just another signal from life we can relate to artfully to guide us towards healthful living) 3/

Few of us have had skillful developmental guidance into symbiotic naturalness, nourishment, and generativity, which at least for the moment are how I personally conceptualize the fundamentals of emotional-relational health 4/

Many of us know extractive forms of naturalness, nourishment, + generativity. For naturalness, the extractive form is performance. Not bad itself, but if we only feel like ourselves when we’re in a role or otherwise ruminating on past or future performance, we’re extracting 5/

For nourishment, the extractive form is intoxication and/or dissociative consumption. For generativity, it’s alienated productivity (productivity without direct ties to the well-being of ourselves and/or those we care about)  6/

With the level of alienation of the current moment, no wonder we have so much difficulty even knowing what we “need.” People make agreements and project into the future based on poorly rooted perceptions, without a healthy ecology of connection supporting attunement 7/
Really , transforming either “neediness,” or the other end of the spectrum -> hyper-independence, is a process of, imho, tending daily to the field within and around you. 8/

The most foundational skill imho is orientation. We’re often unconscious of what we’re oriented to (often it’s performance, production, consumption, control, etc), and when you’re not aware you’re going to take steps in the wrong direction even if the steps “feel good” 9/

Becoming more and more familiar with the way your body-mind-spirit senses when it’s operating from true naturalness, or accessing nourishing states, or creating from a place of sustainable and connected generativity, seems to more reliably create healthfulness ime 10/

This imo is a large part of what makes meditation valuable. If practiced/taught well, it fosters dexterity of mind-body-spirit and you naturally notice your alignment with well-being. Eventually you find that you’re already oriented to it and it becomes subtler and subtler 11/

And subtler until there’s nothing but naturalness, sobriety from the contrived life we thought we should be living, sobriety from manipulating ourselves and others and the world around us, and natural connectedness is abundant 12/
That’s what I got for now at least. Who knows, I could be completely wrong 😌😘😌 /end thread

One of the best pieces of wisdom I ever got was from creative writing: “Write off the subject.”

It was a simple instruction to allow  oneself to take left turns, go wherever feels most interesting/vivid/fun, whether or not it fit neatly into your initial subject or idea 1/ https://t.co/VY9uR7vEaJ

The time’s I’ve drilled myself deep into holes that led to less favorable outcomes is when I've gotten tunnel vision around my "goal" or "role" or "idea," getting anxious when the ground felt uncertain. 2/

Turns out that uncertainty is a gift, that it led to deepening dexterity of my senses for the paths unfolding in this complex human life, and when I allowed for turns and flourishes outside of the tunnel vision frame, I found myself much more vibrant and playful all around 3/
Every endeavor can be a pathway of delight, rather than a chore to serve the tunnel vision idea of who you “should” be or what you “should” do. 4/

My take atm: at times of toxic collective inflammation, you’ve got to tend to inflammation first. Can you actually create non-inflamed pathways of mutuality? Trying to solve sociocultural/political problems while inflamed is like trying to take the SAT with a concussion 1/

Even amongst people with shared views and “solidarity” the actual relational field is typically •fraught• and v disembodied. You want to create a culture that cares for people better than the one we have now? If you can’t do it at micro levels it won’t work at macro levels 2/

This is a big piece of figuring out where mutual agency lives, which requires surrendering to spaces of the unknown and being unknowing together, being willing to be surprised at any moment where connectedness to humanity becomes apparent 3/

People that are trying to create “community” and have “studied” and “practiced” are often just recreating business structures and social scenes and clubs and institutions/schools, or getting off on the intoxicants of practice/study outcomes (confusing orgasm for liberation) 4/

People will put together really cool shit but extract their humanity in various ways to do it because we’re used to extractive modes of operating (having our labor/performance extracted from us; consumption/extracting pleasure from our bodies and/or the world as the reward) 5/

So many of us are so trained into productive and consumptive modes of being that we don’t know what rest and spacious attunement feel like, let alone having the embodied and emergent/dynamic communication that allows for the preventative care/minimization of inflammation 6/

And it’s very critical to deal with inflammation first because if you don’t, the waves and movements that you cultivate will be easily co-opted by inflammatory forces bc people won’t be able to discern inflammatory praxis from mutually nourishing praxis 7/

There’s v much something here about honoring the organic nature of humanity, and that finding true power (as opposed to performing constructed ideas of power) requires living in spaces of authentic uncertainty as opposed to the arrogance of attaching to your conceptualizations 8/

This isn’t to say that you have to rigidly police inflammation; inflammation is a response a healthy nervous system produces. What I’m saying is that inflammation must be met with discernment and grace, and at times of widespread inflammation those are precious resources 1/ https://t.co/0pqpAXpnKE

Acknowledging mutual inflammation and orienting to pathways of mutual well-being v obviously go hand-in-hand, but when operating within a culture of contorted embodiments that’s an incredibly under-developed skillset, the fine motor movements of relationality still rudimentary 2/

People will very quickly go from small experiences of connection to “We should build The Thing™️,” not seeing that they’re trying to dominate/mechanize themselves and their lives into Empowerment™️ when empowerment requires the patient but direct tending of organic factors 3/

AI getting good at psychotherapy is pointing to the very limitations of psychotherapy as a model. The individualized, compartmentalized, and highly cognitive nature of it makes it easy to replicate and replace with LLM/RLHF stuff. Even “somatic” therapists are often just doing 1/

Exercises with clients that will be easy for AI to replicate, if it hasn’t already. Psychotherapy itself as a one-way transactional model (pay x dollars for one unit of healing from a provider who serves you empathy and growth) points to this flaw: 2/

Psychotherapy doesn’t require the therapist to be fully human. Good psychotherapy of course comes from people who are very adept at being fully human while in this role, but that very level of humanity is vanishingly scarce within the professional-managerial class culture that 3/

Psychotherapy emerges from. That shit is a lot of performance, contortion, and socially acceptable manipulation (of both self and other). Exactly what AI is able to replace will point to what needs to emerge for psychotherapy to remain relevant, and imho that something 4/

Has to be much more about mutuality and integration into village and community than the current model is. That’s going to take a level of integrity + practice I don’t think psychotherapy training typically does for people, which instead typically follows institutional models 5/

That prioritize conformity, are slow to adapt, and have been decaying for some time.

Or idk I could be completely wrong 🤭

/end

Fully forgave my dad after experiencing the deepest suffering of my life and getting some brief relief. He died about 6 months later. There had been some recognition before that he was simply emotionally and relationally disabled, but I couldn’t have him in my life. 1/ https://t.co/vo3FEdocBd

My own depth of suffering made it easier to see how he had no choice in his condition, as I became very deeply aware of my own, of the organic + fickle nature of this existence. I had a depth of compassion for this before, but really you can’t know until life makes it visceral 2/

I never set out explicitly to forgive anyone, including myself. I simply set out to cultivate pathways that could yield nourishment and love and spaciousness, and the edges and resentments I had towards myself and others made themselves apparent when they needed to 3/

My dad wasn’t capable of true change, simply didn’t have the capacity for insight or self-compassion (all his attempts at change were driven by incredibly deep insecurities/trauma). But I feel him moving through me every day, and there are gifts within that even when the ugly 4/

Parts of his impact and personality show up. There are lots of takes +  advice for forgiveness out there, many of them helpful. And generally it’s going to be much more visceral and organic than one could imagine, bc forgiveness isn’t a skill or an end, it’s a continuation 5/

Of an ever unfolding process and an integrated aspect of an ongoing relationship. 

That’s my thoughts atm at least

Grains of salt etc

Closest thing to truth I can say is that it’s worth it to authentically reckon with your emotional-relational life, whatever forms that takes

Perhaps the most important piece of wisdom with respect to ignorance in its many forms is that you can never escape ignorance; models of the world, of others, of the human condition, are always filled with approximations and estimates 1/

There’s always things you don’t know that you don’t know. When honoring this you can gain skillfulness that undercuts self-righteousness; the place of finding non-ignorance isn’t about finding concrete truths but about welcoming unknowing deeply 2/

Among the most important things I’ve learned in self-development + mutual support is that the space of mutual unknowing, of comfort with uncertainty, is among the most powerfully transformative places. It allows that which is undoubtable to shine through more clearly 3/

After a lot of reckoning w myself + my sense of the world, I think the only good path to collective change is patiently inviting yourself + those you’re connected with into maturity. And that’s an incredibly humbling practice. 1/ https://t.co/qiKZZfuS4y

Maturity is spacious and creative, attuned and curious. It’s a lot of unknowing, and a lot of sensing into what actual power/agency is operating moment to moment, day to day, relationship to relationship. 2/
If you’ve ever held some responsibility for another’s well-being, it’s awfully humbling both how much and how little power we have. 3/
If you’ve ever tried to change/grow yourself, you find even how little choice we have in this organic life even when we have considerable motivation. 4/
Imho collective change is always a product of organic processes. Are you tending to them patiently or trying to squeeze an acorn into becoming a tree? 5/

This is an important metaphor: you ever try to force a relationship? A project? Even when it works short term it does not produce sustainable results, and often has severe side effects down the road 6/

Those types of things are dynamic and shapeshifting over the course of life, but many of us (speaking from abundant experience here lol) start scrambling anxiously when that dynamism shows up. 2/
Letting go of “filling in the blanks” allows room for wisdom to show up, for not taking yourself too seriously while also taking yourself seriously in a mature fashion. 3/

One hard part is, sometimes much of what we think of as “important beliefs” are actually ways of compulsively filling in blanks about ourselves, our relationships, the world. Do you really believe it if you can’t let there be uncertainty? 4/

My general inclination has been to treat forgiveness less as an action and more as a potential byproduct of a healthy relationship with the pain between self and other (or within the self for self-forgiveness). 1/ https://t.co/yfhaMmvUe5

This is in part influenced by the compulsory forgiveness norms of Catholic/Christian culture, which  brings a lot of baggage to the term, and thus I’d rather break down healthy forgiveness into the component parts instead to avoid that baggage. 2/
There’s also the potential of false forgiveness, which much like manipulative forms of positivity creates precarious well-being that eventually crashes due to poorly constructed foundations. 3/

At its root it comes down to holding everything that arises in relationship with grace, both your experience and your sense of the other(s) in the relational field. Sometimes that’ll mean setting firm boundaries, sometimes that’ll mean staying close even with mixed feelings 4/

I generally orient to where embodied nourishment is available, where one can at minimum feel some sense of naturalness and space even if  interacting with someone (either in real time or in reflection after the fact) who projects pain outwards 5/

The forgiveness process is much about distribution. The need for forgiveness comes from a place where our well-being was in someone’s hands who couldn’t handle their responsibilities towards it, either in a particular instance and/or chronically over time 6/

Forgiveness in part requires re-distribution of responsibility within the relationship; when both people have the capacity for insight + relational reconstruction, you can orient to shared pain + then collaboratively re-calibrate to the just right level of intimacy from there 7/

When one or both people can’t develop insight into the dynamic and/or have capacity for reconstruction, then it’s about boundary setting and distributing responsibility for your well-being elsewhere in the field 8/

Self-forgiveness is about recognizing you weren’t capable at a moment or over time of the responsibility you were taking on or given for the well-being of self/others, and that helps to recalibrate to healthy redistribution of responsibility in your field 9/

Imho it’s a mistake to make forgiveness about having certain thoughts or feelings towards a relationship or person. The underlying foundation from which healthy thoughts and feelings arise is a living, dynamic relationship to our shared responsibility for the relational field 10/

The relational field includes the people involved as well as their intersecting networks and a lot beyond that. This can sound overwhelming but it’s actually freeing because that sense of the greater field attunes you to the ways you are holding and being held 11/

Making it •just• about your thoughts + feelings regarding a specific person or event or sociocultural condition, it’s easy to get tunnel vision and miss the opportunity it points to: the world has many invitations to healthy mutual responsibility, and you can tap into that 12/

When you orient to where well-being is (which is in part about right relationship to wounding/pain), then you naturally find your way towards holding relationships in a way that spacious well-being between people becomes available, and forgiveness reveals itself in due time 13/

That often requires allowing yourself to welcome anger or grief towards self or other, and periods of mixed feelings and/or confusion. Why finding support is often needed is because that shit is hard to hold alone, and why I emphasize distribution here 14/

I’ve seen people create shallower forms of forgiveness of self or others through compulsive performances (eg co-dependency, covering up deep insecurity, etc). That avoids the vulnerable but necessary work of finding the right distribution of connection energy in one’s life imho

When trying to do any change or maintenance work (personal, relational, communal, sociopolitical), you have to cultivate a culture people (incl. yourself) want to participate in. Chastising and coercing isn’t sustainable/doesn’t work, even when you have the “moral high ground” 1/

Your emotional reactions to the visceral and ugly elements of life make sense, but it’s up to you to wield them artistically and in wholesome/nourishing service of the beings and causes you care about 2/

Every human carries the weight of the world and if you’ve judged them as if they don’t, that’s from your own ignorance not their lack of humanity.

Just cuz you can’t perceive their pain doesn’t mean it’s not there.

One thing to reckon with is the fact that our current language and social scripts are so laden with power and assertion of what is true/untrue that nearly every statement is a power move of some type, and it doesn’t have to be this way 1/ https://t.co/NPqTEODVlw

If you’ve done a decent amount of work towards well-being, inner peace, relational wisdom, etc, you’ll often find that there’s compulsive opinion making and belief maintenance processes we’ve internalized that create near constant, extractive workload/tension 2/

Well-being’s pathways reveal themselves naturally when we stop reifying the thought world (without refuting thought, which is just a product of reifying a belief of thoughts as bad), but all our language + social pressures shape us toward treating our thought objects as “real” 3/

One of the biggest fear barriers to finding that which is unconditional, the place of the most abundant compassion and grace, is simply the compulsive need to maintain and attach to beliefs, the constant need to “act” 4/

This isn’t to say you should fight beliefs, or stop yourself from taking action. But unconditional love has no preference for belief or non-belief, action or non-action. Those are conditions. The truly unconditional is always here, always revealing itself 5/

On a positive note, the processes that get in the way exhaust themselves. People develop wisdom through their own organic process. You shouldn’t read my thread as  something you “should” do or think. 6/

After all I’m just some dude. And I could be completely wrong about all this. And talking about the unconditional is one of the silliest things one can do. But I don’t mind being silly.

🤭

7/

There’s a big difference between remembering/reflecting/projecting and •experiencing•, and this presents a core issue in how we orient to nourishment and naturalness and generativity. 1/

Our language and social scripts are built around remembering/projecting/narrativizing and we barely tap into the real time process of being in connection with self and others, what signals our well-being and how to rest in it. Nervous system is subjugated to narrative, roughly 2/

When you reflect on a question of “What’s nourishing to you?” you might think “X gathering/activity the other week was nourishing for me,” but that experience is made up of so many things, and you might have gotten a sense of nourishment from a few brief moments within it 3/

This isn’t at all to say that reflection/projection are bad, but instead that attuning to the elements of good connection with self and others requires fundamental process-based and nervous system-inclusive skills that the current collective moment is missing 4/

There’s simply an •enormous• difference between having a few brief moments of connection and being able to create positive narrative meaning around them vs. being able to orient directly to connectedness and it’s real time emergent processes. 5/

This recognition can become problematic if it becomes an over-reliance on compartmentalized “healing” practices. The healthy ways orient people directly to how they’re situated in a relational ecosystem and cultivate skills to tend to it patiently and curiously 6/
The toxic forms tend to centralize power around practices/scenes/guru-types etc, creating shared language that mostly only works in these compartments removed from everyday life 7/

As always, grains of salt, I am as incredibly fallible as every other human, but I have put an incredible amount of work into understanding the fundamentals of emotional-relational well-being.

All you can do keep getting curious about what feeds you and those you care about /end

More late night thoughts:

In order to live a calling, from an authentic place, you have to live in concert with your human and non-human ecosystem. It’s giving you feedback all the time about what you’re being called into, and if you don’t like the feedback that’s arriving… 1/

Or the available ways of dancing with that feedback, it’s time to re-orient, cuz there’s dis-orientation in the system. That might mean orienting to skill building, or to different aspects of experience/perception, or to different people or paths. 2/

If you feel drawn to a calling and want to do it with integrity, you have to surrender to where integrity comes through you, where it reveals itself most readily, and that means a level of humility for when you’re out of your depth/lane or acting from a place of pain 3/

It’s important to be both serious and not take yourself seriously. To have capacity to be both wholesome and hardcore. And to orient your energy only to where you’re genuinely open to honoring the connection in good faith 4/

When you fully welcome doubt and uncertainty, letting go of agenda, then what emerges comes from that which is undoubtable.
